title: Any prediction markets weather traders here? How does your setup look like?
author: Helloiamvu
selftext:
I started trading Kalshi weather contracts a few months ago with two of my friends. One of us does the quant work, the other two handle the engineering. We've been doing quite well but I'll be honest, we spent way more time wrangling data than actually building the strategy.

The thing nobody tells you about weather trading is how messy the data is. One API reports in Fahrenheit. Another gives you Celsius. Satellite data is in Kelvin. And then you realize how much sources differ. IEM rounds temperatures but raw METAR gives you decimal precision, and the difference matters a lot.

We burned weeks building out the data pipeline before we could even run a proper backtest. Pulling from NOAA, NWS, IEM, GOES satellites. Deduplicating overlapping sources. Matching each contract to the station it actually settles on. Massive amount of work for what should be a simple question: what was the temperature?

After talking to a few other traders we realized everyone is rebuilding the same pipeline from scratch. We've been thinking about cleaning up our internal pipeline and releasing it for free. Right now it covers market prices and order books from both Kalshi and Polymarket, plus observations, forecasts, satellite, and settlement data for every high/low weather contract. Everything matched to the correct settlement station, units reconciled, sources deduplicated. It also has point-in-time queries so your backtests don't leak future data, and a compact output format that cuts token usage by 40-60% if you're running agents or using LLMs. We use it to trade every day.  
  
Curious if anyone else here trades weather. What does your data setup look like? What's the most annoying part?

comments.author: randomcluster
comments.body: I have been working on this, I started r/WeatherTrading to document parts of it. The markets have been growing pretty dramatically over the last year, and volume during the NYC snowstorm last year peaked at nearly $2mm which is serious for such a new market! I have many feeds running and a lot of the links on NOAA are broken after DOGE disruption but there are so many undocumented feeds still available and I did much of the same work you did I'm sure. There are literally 3500+ stations so I can only see more cities being added. I have a family member who is a famous weather researcher so I have taken some findings from their work related to ensemble bias forecasting and extreme weather modeling, which has been really useful.

comments.author: j-smith-92
comments.body: Have recently been looking at weather trading, would be interested in your setup

comments.author: According_King4972
comments.body: Not gonna lie, i wasted weeks on data pipelines too and it barely moved pnl. what actually helped was focusing on decision layer instead of data layer. i made 16% gain on Kalshi after switching to using a chat based AI agent Kumo that compares forecasts + explains confidence instead of just dumping numbers.. way less setup, no APIs, just ask and act

comments.author: CivilStrawberry28
comments.body: The hardest part really is the data normalization. everyone talks about the strategy but not the engineering nightmare behind getting reliable weather inputs in the first place. i’ve been using AI weather analyst Kumo for prediction markets and honestly the consistency of the forecast structure matters more than people think

comments.author: Successful_Pie_1239
comments.body: It's all public data. Honestly, you just need to find the right data source. I've been in the weather business for many years. Happy to help in any ways.

replies.author: Successful_Pie_1239
replies.body: Public models like HRRR, GFS and ECMWF
